title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an acoustic engineer called Abbi Grant talking about the impact of noise in urban environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an acoustic engineer called Abbi Grant talking about the impact of noise in urban environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Abbi Grant discusses how sound design can improve the quality of life in busy cities.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Abbi. When we talk about pollution, we usually mean air or water. But noise pollution is just as damaging to our health. As an acoustic engineer, I study the 'soundscape' of the city. My interest in this field didn't start with a scientific project; it actually began when I visited a [7]grand cathedral[/7] as a child. I was amazed at how the building itself could transform a busy street into a place of perfect silence. I wanted to understand how architecture could control sound.

Constant city noise can lead to high blood pressure and sleep disorders. I’ve found that [8]low-frequency hums[/8] from air conditioning units and traffic are the biggest contributors to urban stress. We are often not even aware of them, but our brains are constantly processing the sound, which prevents us from fully relaxing. We need to design buildings that act as acoustic filters.

One solution is the use of 'acoustic green walls'. I noticed that [9]dense foliage[/9] is much more effective at absorbing sound than a concrete wall. The leaves scatter the sound waves, preventing them from echoing between buildings. Plus, the visual presence of plants has a psychological effect that makes people perceive the remaining noise as less annoying. It’s a win-win for urban design.

Technology is helping us map noise in real-time. We use 'sound cameras' that allow us to see exactly where noise is leaking out of a building. I found that [10]gaps in window frames[/10] are the most common source of noise complaints in residential areas. By fixing these tiny leaks, we can significantly improve the comfort of a home. It’s often the small details that make the biggest difference.

We are also designing 'quiet zones' in public parks. I noticed that [11]water features[/11], like fountains or artificial streams, are great for 'masking' the sound of distant traffic. The sound of moving water is naturally relaxing and it helps to create a psychological barrier between the park and the busy street outside.

However, we have to be careful about the 'silence of the city'. I’m worried that [12]electric vehicles[/12] are now too quiet, which can be dangerous for pedestrians and cyclists. We are actually working on designing 'artificial engine sounds' that are audible enough to be safe, but not so loud that they add to noise pollution. It’s a strange challenge for an engineer.

Looking forward, I believe we will see 'smart glass' that can cancel out noise. I noticed that [13]active noise cancellation[/13] technology, similar to what you find in headphones, is already being built into window prototypes. You could flip a switch and your city apartment would become as quiet as a library. It would revolutionize urban living.

Finally, I’m currently writing a [14]city guide[/14] that highlights the best 'acoustic retreats' in London. I want to help people find places where they can escape the noise and just breathe. Thank you for your time.
